Dear Ms.
Davis:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you by Steven Churchwell concerning the lobbying and gift
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
!
Because
your inquiry is general in nature,
we will treat your letter as a
request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
!
You have inquired whether a statement contained in the
Commission’s current Lobbying Information Manual
(issued in 1990)
concerning the return or reimbursement of gifts from lobbyists and
lobbying firms is consistent with Regulation 18943.
On page
83 of
the manual,
the ollowinginformation is pr-avided:
EXCEPTIONS
TO “GIFT”
DEFINITION
Returned and Reimbursed Gifts:
The term “gift”
does not include gifts which are not used and which
within
30 days after receipt,
are returned or are
1/
Government Code Sections
81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)

Judith Sproul Davis

Page
2
reimbursed to the donor or delivered to a charitable
organization without being claimed as a charitable
contribution for tax purposes.
With respect to the gift prohibition contained in
Coy.
Code Sections 86203 and 86204, to avoid the
appearance of an improper gift,
lobbyists and officials
should make every effort to avoid a situation in which a
lobbyist makes a payment of more than $10 in a calendar
month which benefits an official.
If such a payment is
made,
no violation of the gift prohibition will occur so
long as the lobbyist is reimbursed by. the official
before the end of the calendar month in which the
payment was made; except in the case of a payment made
less than a week prior to the end of the month, in which
case no violation will occur if the lobbyist receives
reimbursement within one week of making the payment.
I have researched this matter and have located a memorandum
from FPPC Chief Counsel Mike Baker dated June 29,
1977
(No.
M-77-
340),
which established this policy for lobbyist gifts.
The
policy has also been set out in the following advice letters:
Eichinan,
No.
A—77-336;
and Eichman,
No.
A—83—210.
Similar
reporting advice was provided for lobbyist employers in Ducicran,
No.
A-83—267.
Copies of the memorandum and letters are enclosed.
However,
we agree that the rule for return, donation,
or
reimbursement of a gift contained in Regulation 18943 should be
applicable to all gifts,
including gifts made by a lobbyist or a
lobbying firm.
Therefore, if a public official reimburses a
lobbyist,
lobbying firm,
or lobbyist employer for the value of a
gift within 30 days of receipt, the gift has no value for purposes
of Sections 86203,
89504,
and 89505,
and need not be disclosed by
any filer
-
.
—
-
-
—
We will make the necessary changes to the Lobbying
Information Manual,
and will announce in the FPPC Bulletin that
the above—referenced memorandum and letters have been superseded.
Thank you for bringing this matter to our attention.
If you
-
have any additional concerns, please do not hesitate to contact me
at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
)
:
By:
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

sections.question: You have inquired whether a statement contained in the Commission's current Lobbying Information Manual (issued in 1990) concerning the return or reimbursement of gifts from lobbyists and lobbying firms is consistent with Regulation 18943.
sections.conclusion: However, we agree that the rule for return, donation, or reimbursement of a gift contained in Regulation 18943 should be applicable to all gifts, including gifts made by a lobbyist or a lobbying firm. Therefore, if a public official reimburses a lobbyist, lobbying firm, or lobbyist employer for the value of a gift within 30 days of receipt, the gift has no value for purposes of Sections 86203, 89504, and 89505, and need not be disclosed by any filer.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the FPPC's Lobbying Information Manual provision regarding return or reimbursement of gifts from lobbyists and lobbying firms is consistent with Regulation 18943.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC agrees that Regulation 18943's 30-day reimbursement rule for gift return or reimbursement should apply to all gifts, including those from lobbyists and lobbying firms. If a public official reimburses a lobbyist or lobbying firm within 30 days of receipt, the gift has no value and need not be disclosed.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter treating request as per Regulation 18329(c). Contains historical references to prior FPPC memorandum (M-77-340) and advice letters (A-77-336, A-83-210, A-83-267). Letterhead shows 'IA FAI1' and appears to have minor OCR artifacts ('ollowinginformation'). No separate QUESTION/CONCLUSION section headers, but content clearly delineates question and conclusion. Document includes footnotes regarding Government Code sections and regulatory references.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have inquired whether a statement contained in the Commission's current Lobbying Information Manual (issued in 1990) concerning the return or reimbursement of gifts from lobbyists and lobbying firms is consistent with Regulation 18943.

CONCLUSION: However, we agree that the rule for return, donation, or reimbursement of a gift contained in Regulation 18943 should be applicable to all gifts, including gifts made by a lobbyist or a lobbying firm. Therefore, if a public official reimburses a lobbyist, lobbying firm, or lobbyist employer for the value of a gift within 30 days of receipt, the gift has no value for purposes of Sections 86203, 89504, and 89505, and need not be disclosed by any filer.

Because your inquiry is general in nature, we will treat your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c). 2 ! You have inquired whether a statement contained in the Commission’s current Lobbying Information Manual (issued in 1990) concerning the return or reimbursement of gifts from lobbyists and lobbying firms is consistent with Regulation 18943. With respect to the gift prohibition contained in Coy. Code Sections 86203 and 86204, to avoid the appearance of an improper gift, lobbyists and officials should make every effort to avoid a situation in which a lobbyist makes a payment of more than $10 in a calendar month which benefits an official. If such a payment is made, no violation of the gift prohibition will occur so long as the lobbyist is reimbursed by. the official before the end of the calendar month in which the payment was made; except in the case of a payment made less than a week prior to the end of the month, in which case no violation will occur if the lobbyist receives reimbursement within one week of making the payment. I have researched this matter and have located a memorandum from FPPC Chief Counsel Mike Baker dated June 29, 1977 (No. M-77- 340), which established this policy for lobbyist gifts. The policy has also been set out in the following advice letters: Eichinan, No. A—77-336; and Eichman, No. A—83—210. However, we agree that the rule for return, donation, or reimbursement of a gift contained in Regulation 18943 should be applicable to all gifts, including gifts made by a lobbyist or a lobbying firm. Therefore,
embedding.summary: The FPPC provides informal advice confirming and clarifying its policy on the return or reimbursement of gifts from lobbyists and lobbying firms, agreeing that Regulation 18943's 30-day reimbursement rule applies to all gifts including those from lobbyists. The FPPC commits to updating its Lobbying Information Manual and superseding prior memoranda and advice letters on this matter.
